theorems.title: Dichotomy bound for feedforward architectures
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(F\) be a feedforward architecture generated by an underlying graph \(G\) with \(N\geq2\) computation nodes and \(F_i\) be the class of functions associated with computation node \(n_i\) of \(G\), \(1\leq i\leq N\). Let \(d=\sum_{i=1}^N\operatorname{VCdim}(F_i)\). Then

\[\Delta_F(m)\leq\prod_{i=1}^N\Delta_{F_i}(m)\leq(Nem/d)^d\]

for \(m\geq d\), where \(e\) is the base of the natural logarithm.
theorems.proofHtml:
Assume \(G\) has \(n\) input nodes and that the computation nodes of \(G\) are ordered so that node \(n_i\) receives inputs only from input nodes and from computation nodes \(n_j\), \(1\leq j\leq i-1\). Let \(S\) be a set of \(m\) points in \(\mathbb R^n\). The dichotomy induced on \(S\) by the function in node \(n_1\) can be chosen in at most \(\Delta_{F_1}(m)\) ways. This choice determines the input to node \(n_2\) for each of the \(m\) points in \(S\). The dichotomy induced on these \(m\) inputs by the function in node \(n_2\) can be chosen in at most \(\Delta_{F_2}(m)\) ways, etc. Any dichotomy of \(S\) induced by the whole network can be obtained by choosing dichotomies for each of the \(n_i\)’s in this manner, hence

\[\Delta_F(m)\leq\prod_{i=1}^N\Delta_{F_i}(m).\]

By a theorem of Sauer [sau72], whenever \(\operatorname{VCdim}(F)=k<\infty\), \(\Delta_F(m)\leq(em/k)^k\) for all \(m\geq k\) (see also [behw87b]). Let \(d_i=\operatorname{VCdim}(F_i)\), \(1\leq i\leq N\). Thus \(d=\sum_{i=1}^N d_i\). Then

\[\prod_{i=1}^N\Delta_{F_i}(m)\leq\prod_{i=1}^N(em/d_i)^{d_i}\]

for \(m\geq d\). Using the fact that \(\sum_{i=1}^N-\alpha_i\log\alpha_i\leq\log N\) whenever \(\alpha_i>0\), \(1\leq i\leq N\), and \(\sum_{i=1}^N\alpha_i=1\), and setting \(\alpha_i=d_i/d\), it is easily verified that \(\prod_{i=1}^N d_i^{d_i}\geq(d/N)^d\). Hence \(\prod_{i=1}^N(em/d_i)^{d_i}\leq(Nem/d)^d\).

theorems.title: Weight-based capacity bounds
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(F\) be the class of all functions computed by feedforward nets defined on a fixed underlying graph \(G\) with \(E\) edges and \(N\geq2\) computation nodes, each of which computes a linear threshold function. Let \(W=E+N\) (the total number of weights in the network, including one weight per edge and one threshold per computation node). Then \(\Delta_F(m)\leq(Nem/W)^W\) for all \(m\geq W\) and

\[\operatorname{VCdim}(F)\leq2W\log(eN).\]
theorems.proofHtml: The first inequality follows from directly from Theorem 1 using the fact that \(\operatorname{VCdim}(F)=k+1\) when \(F\) is the class of all linear threshold functions on \(\mathbb R^k\) (see e.g. [wd81]). For the second inequality, it is easily verified that for \(N\geq2\) and \(m=2W\log(eN)\), \((Nem/W)^W<2^m\). Hence this is an upper bound on \(\operatorname{VCdim}(F)\).

theorems.title: Uniform relative-deviation bound
theorems.proofNote: The paper quotes this theorem from Vapnik [vap82] and gives no proof. Its footnote states the required measurability qualification.
theorems.statementHtml:
[vap82] (see [behw87b], Theorem A3.3). Let \(F\) be a class of functions on \(\mathbb R^n\), \(0<\gamma\leq1\), \(0<\epsilon,\delta<1\). Let \(S\) be a random sequence of \(m\) examples drawn independently according to the distribution \(D\). The probability that there exists a function in \(F\) that disagrees with at most a fraction \((1-\gamma)\epsilon\) of the examples in \(S\) and yet has error greater than \(\epsilon\) (w.r.t. \(D\)) is less than

\[8\Delta_F(2m)e^{-\gamma^2\epsilon m/4}.\]

We assume some measurability conditions on the class \(F\). See [pol84], [behw87b] for details.

theorems.title: Sample bound for linear threshold networks
theorems.statementHtml:
Given a fixed graph \(G\) with \(E\) edges and \(N\) linear threshold units (i.e. \(W=E+N\) weights), fixed \(0<\epsilon\leq1/2\), and \(m\) random training examples, where

\[m\geq\frac{32W}{\epsilon}\ln\frac{32N}{\epsilon},\]

if one can find a choice of weights so that at least a fraction \(1-\epsilon/2\) of the \(m\) training examples are correctly loaded, then one has confidence at least \(1-8e^{-1.5W}\) that the net will correctly classify all but a fraction \(\epsilon\) of future examples drawn from the same distribution. For

\[m\geq\frac{64W}{\epsilon}\ln\frac{64N}{\epsilon},\]

the confidence is at least \(1-8e^{-\epsilon m/32}\).
theorems.proofHtml:
Let \(\gamma=1/2\) and apply Theorem 3, using the bound on \(\Delta_F(m)\) given in Corollary 2. This shows that the probability that there exists a choice of the weights that defines a function with error greater than \(\epsilon\) that is consistent with at least a fraction \(1-\epsilon/2\) of the training examples is at most

\[8(2Nem/W)^W e^{-\epsilon m/16}.\]

When \(m=(32W/\epsilon)\ln(32N/\epsilon)\), this is

\[8\left(2e\frac{\epsilon}{32N}\ln\frac{32N}{\epsilon}\right)^W,\]

which is less than \(8e^{-1.5W}\) for \(N\geq2\) and \(\epsilon\leq1/2\). When \(m\geq(64W/\epsilon)\ln(64N/\epsilon)\), \((2Nem/W)^W\leq e^{\epsilon m/32}\), so

\[8(2Nem/W)^W e^{-\epsilon m/16}\leq8e^{-\epsilon m/32}.\]

theorems.title: Sample bound for reduced architectures
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(F\) be the class of all functions computed by linear threshold feedforward nets defined on a fixed underlying graph \(G\) with \(N'\geq2\) computation nodes and \(E'\geq N'\) edges, such that at most \(E\geq2\) edges have non-zero weights and at most \(N\geq2\) nodes have at least one incoming edge with a non-zero weight. Let \(W=E+N\). Then the conclusion of Corollary 4 holds for sample size

\[m\geq\frac{32W}{\epsilon}\ln\frac{32NE'}{\epsilon}.\]
theorems.proofHtml:
We can bound \(\Delta_F(m)\) by considering the number of ways the \(N\) nodes and \(E\) edges that remain can be chosen from among those in the initial network. A crude upper bound is \((N')^N(E')^E\). Applying Corollary 2 to the remaining network gives

\[\Delta_F(m)\leq(N')^N(E')^E(Nem/W)^W.\]

This is at most \((NE'em/W)^W\). The rest of the analysis is similar to that in Corollary 4.

theorems.title: Distribution-free sample lower bound
theorems.proofNote: The paper quotes this result from [ehkv87] (and points also to [behw87b]) without reproducing its proof.
theorems.statementHtml:
[ehkv87] (see also [behw87b]). Let \(F\) be a class of \(\{-1,+1\}\)-valued functions on \(\mathbb R^n\) with \(\operatorname{VCdim}(F)\geq2\). Let \(A\) be any learning algorithm that takes as input a sequence of \(\{-1,+1\}\)-labeled examples over \(\mathbb R^n\) and produces as output a function from \(\mathbb R^n\) into \(\{-1,+1\}\). Then for any \(0<\epsilon\leq1/8\), \(0<\delta\leq1/100\) and

\[m<\max\left[\frac{1-\epsilon}{\epsilon}\ln\frac1\delta,\frac{\operatorname{VCdim}(F)-1}{32\epsilon}\right],\]

there exists (1) a function \(f\in F\) and (2) a distribution \(D\) on \(\mathbb R^n\times\{-1,+1\}\) for which \(\operatorname{Prob}((\vec x,a):a\neq f(\vec x))=0\), such that given a random sample of size \(m\) chosen according to \(D\), with probability at least \(\delta\), \(A\) produces a function with error greater than \(\epsilon\).

theorems.title: VC lower bound for one-hidden-layer networks
theorems.proofNote: The paper presents this theorem as a corollary of the slicing construction in [b88] and gives no separate proof.
theorems.statementHtml: The class of one hidden layer linear threshold nets taking input from \(\mathbb R^n\) with \(k\) hidden units has VC dimension at least \(2\lfloor k/2\rfloor n\).

theorems.title: Random hypercube sets are shattered
theorems.statementHtml:
With probability approaching 1 exponentially in \(n\), a set \(S\) of \(m\leq2^{n/3}\) vectors chosen randomly and uniformly from \(\{+1,-1\}^n\) can be shattered by the one hidden layer architecture with

\[2\left\lceil\frac{m}{\left\lfloor n\left(1-\frac{10}{\ln n}\right)\right\rfloor}\right\rceil\]

linear threshold units in its hidden layer.
theorems.proofHtml:
With probability approaching 1 exponentially in \(n\) no pair of vectors in \(S\) are negations of each other. Assume \(n\geq e^{10}\). Let \(r=\lfloor n(1-10/(\ln n))\rfloor\). Divide \(S\) at random into \(\lceil m/r\rceil\) disjoint subsets \(S_1,\ldots,S_{\lceil m/r\rceil}\) each containing no more than \(r\) vectors. We will describe a set \(T\) of \(\pm1\) vectors as sliceable if the vectors in \(T\) are linearly independent and the subspace they span over the reals does not contain any \(\pm1\) vector other than the vectors in \(T\) and their negations. In [odl88] it is shown, for large \(n\), that any random set of \(r\) vectors has probability

\[P=4\binom r3\left(\frac34\right)^n+O\!\left(\left(\frac7{10}\right)^n\right)\]

of not being sliceable. Thus the probability that some \(S_i\) is not sliceable is \(O(mn^2(3/4)^n)\), which is exponentially small for \(m\leq2^{n/3}\). Hence with probability approaching 1 exponentially in \(n\), each \(S_i\) is sliceable, \(1\leq i\leq\lceil m/r\rceil\).

Consider any Boolean function \(f\) on \(S\) and let \(S_i^+=\{\vec x\in S_i:f(\vec x)=+1\}\), \(1\leq i\leq\lceil m/r\rceil\). If \(S_i\) is sliceable and no pair of vectors in \(S\) are negations of each other then we may pass a plane through the points in \(S_i^+\) that doesn’t contain any other points in \(S\). Shifting this plane parallel to itself slightly we can construct two half spaces whose intersection forms a slice of \(\mathbb R^n\) containing \(S_i^+\) and no other points in \(S\). Using threshold units at the hidden layer recognizing these two half spaces, with weights to the output unit \(+1\) and \(-1\) appropriately, the output unit receives input \(+2\) for any point in the slice and \(0\) for any point not in the slice. Doing this for each \(S_i^+\) and thresholding at \(1\) implements the function \(f\).

theorems.title: Sample lower bounds for neural-network learning
theorems.proofNote: The paper introduces this corollary as an application of Theorem 6; its two bounds use the architecture lower bounds in Theorems 7 and 8, but no separate proof is given.
theorems.statementHtml:
For any learning algorithm training a net with \(k\) linear threshold functions in its hidden layer, and \(0<\epsilon\leq1/8\), if the algorithm uses (a) fewer than

\[\frac{2\lfloor k/2\rfloor n-1}{32\epsilon}\]

examples to learn a function from \(\mathbb R^n\) to \(\{-1,+1\}\), or (b) fewer than

\[\frac{\left\lfloor n\lfloor k/2\rfloor\left(\max\left(1/2,1-10/(\ln n)\right)\right)\right\rfloor-1}{32\epsilon}\]

examples to learn a function from \(\{-1,+1\}^n\) to \(\{-1,+1\}\), for \(k\leq O(2^{n/3})\), then there exist distributions \(D\) for which (i) there exists a choice of weights such that the network exactly classifies its inputs according to \(D\), but (ii) the learning algorithm will have probability at least \(.01\) of finding a choice of weights which in fact has error greater than \(\epsilon\).
